texts.label: text
texts.orig: The consumption of fossil natural gas, which currently serves as the world ' s main energy source, is as large as 4 × 10 12 Nm 3 /year, corresponding to 4.5 × 10 4 Twh, and anticipated to rise by 1.6-2.0% by 2040 [1,2]. Biogas, a renewable alternative, has the potential to cover around 30% of the requirements in the future [3]. In general, natural gas and biogas contains 2-25% and 25-50% CO2, respectively. The CO2  causes pipeline corrosion and reduces the calorific value and therefore, CO2 removal is crucial in natural gas and biogas processing [4].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Physical absorption and adsorption, cryogenic fractionation, chemical-looping combustion, and amine absorption are widely used techniques for CO2 separation in industry. However, these methods have numerous drawbacks, for instance high energy use, complex systems, large footprint, and risk of environmental pollution. Membrane separation, as an emerging technology, does not display these drawbacks [5 -7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, DDR membranes with a layer thickness of approximately 700 nm were studied for separation feeds comprising mixtures of CO2 and CH4. The membranes displayed the highest CO2 over CH4 permselectivity and CO2 permeability reported in literature. This was ascribed to a defect-free and ultra-thin zeolite film as well as an open and highly permeable support. For equimolar mixtures, the highest CO2  over CH4  permselectivity of 727 was observed when the pressure at the feed side was 5 bar(a) and the permeate pressure was 1 bar(a) at 25 ◦ C. At these conditions, the CO2  permeability was very high at 45 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa). Separation experiments for 80/20 and 20/80 mixtures were also performed, and in these cases, CO2 over CH4 permselectivities of 1011 and 622  were  observed,  respectively.  For  all  feeds,  the  membrane  permselectivity  decreased  slightly  at  higher temperature and in all cases, higher permselectivity was observed when vacuum was applied at the permeate side. One-stage membrane processes for upgrading biogas to biomethane at three different operating pressures were designed based on the experimental data. In all cases, a quite low membrane area, methane slip and power need were observed.

texts.orig: Zeolites  are  crystalline  microporous  materials  with  quite  uniform pores. Since only molecules smaller than the pore diameter may pass through the pores, zeolites may separate molecules through molecular sieving. Due to the different affinities with different substances, zeolites are  also  employed  as  adsorbents.  In  addition,  zeolites  display  high thermal, mechanical and chemical stability. Because of these three facts, zeolites are promising as membrane material. Consequently, in the past decades, zeolite membranes have been developed and investigated in liquid and gas separations, membrane reactors, and sensors [7].

texts.orig: CHA  and  DDR  zeolites  are  promising  membrane  materials  for removal of CO2 from CH4. In CHA zeolite, the pores are circular with a diameter of 0.37 nm, while the kinetic diameter of the CH4  and CO2 molecules are 0.38 nm and 0.33 nm, respectively. CHA membranes with CO2 over CH4 separation permselectivity between 20 and 600 have been reported  [10 -16].  However,  the  observed  CO2 permeability  for  the membranes were quite low. By far, our group reported the highest CO2 permeability ( > 50 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa)) for CHA membranes. At the same time, very high CO2 over CH4 separation selectivities (up to 300) were observed [17 -20]. Recently,  a  CO2/CH4  separation  selectivity  of  as  much  as  1172  was observed for a DDR membrane, however, the observed CO2 permeance was only 8.9 × 10 8 mol/(m 2 s Pa) [29]. Hollow fibre DDR membranes displayed  a  good  CO2 over  CH4 separation  permselectivity  of  447; meanwhile, a higher CO2 permeability of 1.6 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) was observed [22]. The low permeability of the reported membranes probably resulted from a thick zeolite layer, normally thicker than 5 μ m. A high CO2 permeability of 3.2 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) and a CO2 over CH4 separation  permselectivity  of  540  were  however  published  for  DDR membranes with preferentially oriented crystals [23]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, we evaluate very thin (700 nm) DDR membranes for separation of CO2 from CH4. The permeability for pure components and CO2/CH4 mixtures  with  different  compositions  were  evaluated  at various  feed  pressures  and  temperatures.  Based  on  the  experimental data, simple membrane processes for biogas upgrading were designed, and  the  membrane  area,  methane  slip  and  electricity  use  were estimated.
texts.text: In this study, we evaluate very thin (700 nm) DDR membranes for separation of CO2 from CH4. The permeability for pure components and CO2/CH4 mixtures  with  different  compositions  were  evaluated  at various  feed  pressures  and  temperatures.  Based  on  the  experimental data, simple membrane processes for biogas upgrading were designed, and  the  membrane  area,  methane  slip  and  electricity  use  were estimated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ZeoMem Sweden AB supplied DDR membranes discs with a thickness of 3 mm and a diameter of 25 mm. The company requires that details of the membrane preparation are kept confidential, however, the membranes were prepared by seeding and masking of supports as described in a patent [31]. The membranes were sealed in Wicke-Kallenbach cells using rubber O-rings, and the functional area of the membrane was 2.8 cm 2 .

The membranes were sealed in Wicke-Kallenbach cells using rubber O-rings, and the functional area of the membrane was 2.8 cm 2 .

texts.orig: An X-ray diffractometer (Empyrean model from PANalytical equipped with Cu a HR X-ray tube with long fine focus and a PIXcel3D detector)  was  used  to  record  X-ray  diffraction  data  (XRD)  of  the membranes. The microstructure of the membranes was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). A high-resolution field emission instrument (Magellan 400 model from FEI) at LUMIA (Luleå Material Imaging and Analysis) was used for the purpose. The probe current was 6 pA and the accelerating voltage was 3 kV and no conductive coating was used.

texts.orig: 2.3. Process design
texts.text: 2.3. Process design

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One-stage  membrane  processes  for  CO2 removal  from  a  feed comprising a 50/50 mixture to obtain a retentate comprising a 4/96 mixture  were  designed  based  on  the  experimental  data.  Processes operating at 5, 10 and 20 bar at the feed side of the membranes were designed for a feed flow of 24 000 Nm 3 /day. A mathematical model of tubular  membranes  consisting  of  necessary  increments  of  constant membrane area was used to estimate the total membrane area. The CO2 and CH4 permeabilities were assumed to be constant, and the same as the experimentally observed permeabilities. The composition of the feed to each increment was calculated by a mass balance.
texts.text: One-stage  membrane  processes  for  CO2 removal  from  a  feed comprising a 50/50 mixture to obtain a retentate comprising a 4/96 mixture  were  designed  based  on  the  experimental  data.  Processes operating at 5, 10 and 20 bar at the feed side of the membranes were designed for a feed flow of 24 000 Nm 3 /day. A mathematical model of tubular  membranes  consisting  of  necessary  increments  of  constant membrane area was used to estimate the total membrane area. The CO2 and CH4 permeabilities were assumed to be constant, and the same as the experimentally observed permeabilities. The composition of the feed to each increment was calculated by a mass balance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  X-ray  diffraction  pattern  in  Fig.  1a  of  the  DDR  membrane showed that the film comprised a pure DDR phase as evident by comparison with the XRD pattern (ICDD reference code: 00-055-0744) reported for pure DDR crystals [35]. No other reflections were observed. Accordingly, SEM micrographs of the fracture section of the membranes shown in Fig. 1b showed that the thickness of the zeolite membranes was constant and approximately 700 nm. Moreover, the SEM micrographs of the top surface shown in Fig. 1c illustrated that the zeolite films were even. No faults could be observed by careful inspection of the micrographs. In addition, the support pores were clean, which is important to reach high permeability of the membrane.

texts.orig: Fig. 3 shows the permeation data for feed of a 50/50 mixture at a flow rate of 60 Ndm 3 /min for varying pressure at the feed side of the membrane, while the temperature and pressure at the permeate side of the membrane was kept constant at 25 ◦ C and 1 bar(a), respectively. In this case, the highest observed CO2 over CH4 separation permselectivity was quite high at 727 at 5 bar(a), while the CO2 permeability was 45 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 s  Pa).  The  observed  CO2 permeability  for  the  50/50 mixture was more than 60 times higher than the reported results in the literature at the same feed pressure, and the CO2  over CH4  separation permselectivity  observed  in  the  present  work  was  also  much  higher (Table 2). The observed CO2  over CH4  separation permselectivity was lower at higher feed pressure in the present work. However, still, the CO2 over CH4 separation permselectivity was quite high at 424 at a feed pressure of 20 bar(a), which is close to the permselectivity observed at low feed pressure as reported previously. At 20 bar(a), the CO2 permeability was also high, about 17 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) in this study. The highest separation factor of 453 was observed at 10 bar(a) while the CO2 flux was quite high at 1.0 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s). In this case, the separation factor was lower than the observed CO2 over CH4 permselectivity, as a result of the  limited  pressure  ratio.  Consequently,  at  the  highest  investigated pressure  at  the  feed  side  of  the  membrane,  and  thereby  the  highest pressure ratio (20/1), the observed separation factor was 373, which was close to the observed CO2 over CH4 permselectivity of 424. Fig. 3c shows that the observed CO2  flux increased nearly linearly when the pressure at the feed side increased and reached as much as 1.51 mol/ (m 2 ⋅ s) at 20 bar(a).

texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows the separation data recorded while varying the temperature between 25 and 53 ◦ C, while keeping the pressure at the feed and permeate sides of the membrane constant at 10 bar(a) and 1 bar(a), respectively. The CO2 over CH4 separation permselectivity was as high as 593 at 25 ◦ C and it decreased to 348 at the highest temperature of 53 ◦ C. The decrease in permselectivity with the increase in temperature probably resulted from the lower surface permeability permselectivity. Our previous study about the selective mass transport of CO2 over CH4 in MFI and CHA zeolite membranes showed that the activation energy of the surface permeability of CH4  was much higher compared to that of CO2 [36]. Therefore, also in DDR membranes, the mass transport of CH4 will be favoured to a greater extent at higher temperature, which results in  a  lower  surface  permeability  permselectivity.  Still,  the  observed separation permselectivity of 348 at 53 ◦ C was higher than most of the reported values of the permselectivity at low temperature (Table 2). A high separation factor of 269 was measured at 53 ◦ C, and the corresponding CO2  flux of 0.9 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s) was also just slightly lower than that at 25 ◦ C of 1.0 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s).

texts.orig: The separation permselectivity was high at 1011 at 25 ◦ C coupled with a permeability of 23 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) for CO2. At the same time, the separation factor was 861 and the CO2  flux was 1.58 mol/ (m 2 ⋅ s). The high membrane permselectivity and CO2 flux, as compared to the 50/50 mixture, resulted from the high CO2  concentration that reduce  the  surface  barrier  of  CO2 molecules  at  the  feed  side,  which increased the surface permeability of CO2 and the surface permeability permselectivity [36]. The separation permselectivity was reduced to 471 when the temperature increased to 53 ◦ C, and the corresponding CO2 permeability reduced slightly to 21 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa). Permeation data for feeds with high CO2 concentration has rarely been reported, and consequently, there is no data at similar conditions to compare with. However, the separation permselectivity observed at 53 ◦ C in this study was much higher than the reported separation permselectivity for a feed comprising a 90/10 mixture at 24 ◦ C (Table 2).

texts.orig: Permeation data for a feed comprising a 20/80 mix was also recorded in the temperature range 25 - 53 ◦ C while the pressure at the feed and permeate sides of the membrane were kept constant at 10 bar(a) and 1 bar(a),  respectively  (see  Fig.  6).  The  measured  separation  permselectivity was quite high at 622, while the permeability for CO2 was 28 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa) at 25 ◦ C. The separation permselectivity was lower at higher temperatures, but still as high as 417 at 53 ◦ C. At the same time, the CO2 permeability was 24 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 s Pa). As discussed above,  the  decreased  permselectivity  with  increased  temperature resulted from the high activation energy of the surface permeability of CH4, which favoured the mas transport of CH4  to a greater extent at higher  temperatures,  which  leads  to  decreased  surface  permeability permselectivity at higher temperatures. The separation factor was 287 at 25 ◦ C, and the corresponding CO2 flux was 0.26 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s). The separation factor decreased to 198 when the temperature increased to 53 ◦ C, and at the same time, the CO2 flux was slightly decreased to 0.23 mol/ (m 2 ⋅ s). In any case, the measured permeability, flux, separation permselectivity and separation factor for a 20/80 mixture in the current work was much higher than the literature values for DDR membranes, for instance for a 10/90 mixture (Table 2).

texts.orig: Table  2  summarises  the  reported  permeation  data  of  DDR  membranes for CO2/CH4  mixtures. Generally, the membranes evaluated in the  current  study  showed  significantly  higher  CO2  permeability  and slightly higher permselectivities than that reported in the literature. For instance, the CO2 permeability was more than 10 times higher and the separation permselectivity was also slightly higher. In addition, the high feed pressures investigated in the present work, are relevant for industrial applications.

texts.orig: Fig. 7a illustrates a process based on DDR membranes operated at 5 bar(a)  at  the  feed  side.  The  figure  illustrates  that  an  area  of  20  m 2 membranes was sufficient, and the methane loss was only 0.76%. The methane loss was much lower than that reported for commercial water scrubber plants, where the methane loss was as high as 1.97% [37]. By assuming the same module design as in previous work [18], i.e., 43 single channel membrane tubes (1.2 m in length for each tube) and an area of 1.12 m 2 in each module, 18 modules would be sufficient in this case. A membrane of this high quality with a length of 1.2 m seems feasible, since ZeoMem Sweden AB now is marketing 0.5 m long DDR, CHA  and  MFI  membranes.  The  estimated  power  need  was  0.13 kWh/Nm 3 raw biogas including compression of the biomethane (see Table 3). To enable comparison of membrane processes operating at different feed pressures, the power need excluding the compression of biomethane was also estimated as described in previous work [18]. For instance, a power of 0.05 kWh/Nm 3 raw biogas is needed to compress only the biomethane part from 1 to 5 bar(a) [18]. Therefore, the power needed for only CO2 removal from the raw biogas was as low as 0.08 = (0.13-0.05) kWh/Nm 3 raw biogas. This power need was 71% lower than that for a polymeric membrane process operated at the same pressure at the feed side and 43% lower than that for a CHA zeolite membrane process with re-cycle [18].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our previous study showed that two-stage zeolite membrane processes equipped with very permeable and quite selective CHA zeolite membranes could  significantly  reduce  the  costs  and  space  for  membranes and modules and electricity use for biogas upgrading compared to  polymeric membranes [18]. In the present work, we showed that one-stage  DDR  zeolite  membrane  processes  without  re-cycle  can  be designed  with  low  methane  loss  and  with  even  lower  electricity consumption.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To  summarise,  the  DDR  membranes  investigated  here  displayed excellent permeation properties for pure components and mixtures. The membranes showed high selectivities and permeabilities towards CO2 at all  studied conditions. The high CO2  permeability, much higher than reported in the literature, was attributed to the thin (700 nm) and selective DDR layer and clean support. Based on the separation data, it was shown  that  very  compact  one-stage  zeolite  membrane  processes, without recycle and with very low electricity use, were sufficient for efficient  upgrading  of  biogas  to  biomethane.  The  results  further demonstrate that the highly selective and permeable zeolite membranes are competitive materials for industrial upgrading of natural gas and biogas.
